root_text.sn47.34:1.1: “Cattārome, bhikkhave, satipaṭṭhānā bhāvitā bahulīkatā apārā pāraṁ gamanāya saṁvattanti.
root_text.sn47.34:2.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;
root_text.sn47.34:2.5: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.
root_text.sn47.34:2.6: Ime kho, bhikkhave, cattāro satipaṭṭhānā bhāvitā bahulīkatā apārā pāraṁ gamanāya saṁvattantī”ti.

translation_text.sn47.34:1.1: “Mendicants, when these four kinds of mindfulness meditation are developed and cultivated they lead to going from the near shore to the far shore.
translation_text.sn47.34:2.2: It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.34:2.3: They meditate observing an aspect of feelings …
translation_text.sn47.34:2.5: principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.34:2.6: When these four kinds of mindfulness meditation are developed and cultivated they lead to going from the near shore to the far shore.”
